headline_summary: UK hosts virtual talks with ~35 countries on reopening the Strait of Hormuz after a ceasefire; mines and tanker protection discussed (Reuters, Apr 2)
Trump vows more aggressive strikes on Iran over the next 2-3 weeks; oil jumps, stocks slide and USD strengthens (Reuters, Apr 2)
France's Macron says opening Strait of Hormuz by force is unrealistic; calls for consultation with Iran (Reuters, Apr 2)
Philippines says Iran assured safe passage for Philippine-flagged vessels and seafarers through Hormuz (Reuters, Apr 2)
South Korea denies considering paying Iran transit fees for Hormuz passage amid supply concerns (Reuters, Apr 2)
IEA chief warns Middle East oil supply disruptions will rise in April; diesel/jet shortages flagged (Reuters, Apr 1)
Bahrain circulates revised UN Security Council draft on protecting shipping around Hormuz; obstacles remain (Reuters, Apr 1)
US LNG exports hit a record in March as Middle East war disrupts global supply (Reuters, Apr 1)
Dubai crude benchmark faces deliverability/pricing stress as Hormuz export halts disrupt grades (Reuters, Apr 1)
OPEC oil output plunged in March as war forced export cuts, Reuters survey finds (Reuters, Mar 31)
risk_drivers: Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut or severely constrained, elevating global crude/refined product risk premia
Escalation risk from US threats of intensified strikes on Iran, including potential strikes on energy infrastructure
No unified mandate/enforcement for maritime protection despite UN and coalition discussions
Refined product and LNG market tightness amplifies inflation risk and volatility
Policy uncertainty and mixed signals complicate risk pricing and keep volatility elevated

Energy: Higher and more volatile oil prices with upside tail-risk while Hormuz access and minesweeping remain unresolved.
Refined-product cracks (diesel/jet) and LNG spreads likely stay elevated on supply tightness and rerouting costs.
FX: Risk-off episodes and higher energy import bills can pressure EM FX; USD tends to benefit during acute escalation.
Oil-exporter FX sensitivity is more policy/flow-driven than terms-of-trade while exports are constrained by shipping disruptions.
Rates: Energy-driven inflation risk can lift breakevens and term premia; uncertainty can widen credit spreads and keep easing expectations cautious.
Safe-haven demand can compress core yields intraday even as risk premia rise in credit/EM funding markets.
Equities: Energy and defense can outperform; airlines, transport, chemicals, and energy-intensive sectors face margin pressure.
Higher geopolitical risk premium weighs on EM equities and global cyclicals; elevated volatility supports defensive tilts.

sources.title: UK to host talks with 35 countries on reopening Strait of Hormuz
Hopes dim for swift end to Iran war after Trump speech, oil prices surge anew
Macron says it is unrealistic to open Hormuz Strait by force
Iran to allow safe passage of Philippine ships, fuel supply through Strait of Hormuz, says Manila
South Korea denies report it is considering paying Iran Hormuz transit fees
Middle East oil supply disruptions to rise in April and hit Europe, IEA chief says
Bahrain's Hormuz resolution runs into fresh obstacles at UN
